sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 26, level 1.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks on position - collects green blocks VERIFIED(step 2)', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns; can trigger board reconfiguration VERIFIED(step 14)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks, collectable by MOUSE', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'purple (left) + white (right) at row 63, likely progress/timer'}
- Cross-level notes: Green blocks respawn after board reconfiguration. Multiple phases possible within a level.
Current level:
- World model: Level 1, phase 2. Board reconfigured. 9 green blocks in 2 groups: 3 in a row (rows 13-17, cols 25-41) and 6 in 2x3 grid (rows 31-41, cols 13-29). Player at cols 50-52 in bottom sky blue region.
- Goal model: Collect all green blocks by clicking them, then move player to trigger next phase
- Recent findings: ['Board reconfigured after moving RIGHT 3 times (step 14)', '9 green blocks appeared in new positions', 'Player moved to cols 50-52', 'Sky blue now split into 2 regions: top (rows 1-29) and bottom (rows 37-62)', 'Purple bar expanded from 8 to 13 pixels']
- Open questions: ['How many phases total?', 'Does collecting all blocks in each phase advance the game?', 'What triggers board reconfiguration?']
- Plan: Collect all 9 green blocks by clicking their centers
- Strategy log: ['Moving player RIGHT triggered board change - may be intentional mechanic']
- Failed probes: ['ACTION7 - unknown action']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 25 actions; a strong score needs about 21 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board didn't change after moving RIGHT 3 more times. The player is still in the same position and the board looks the same. This means moving RIGHT doesn't always trigger a reconfiguration - it seems to only happen once per phase.
Looking at the current state:
- All green blocks collected in phase 2
- Player moved RIGHT 3 times but no board change
- The player seems to be at the right edge of the sky blue area
Let me think about what else could complete the level:
1. Maybe I need to move LEFT to go back to the starting area
2. Maybe there's a specific position I need to reach
3. Maybe I need to use MOUSE on something else
4. Maybe the level completes when I reach a certain location
Looking at the image, the player is near the right edge of the sky blue region. The sky blue region has a winding shape. Maybe I need to navigate to a specific spot.
Let me try moving LEFT to see if going back triggers something, or if there's a goal at the left side.
Actually, wait - let me reconsider the game mechanics. In phase 1, I collected green blocks and then moved RIGHT which triggered a reconfiguration. In phase 2, I collected green blocks but moving RIGHT didn't trigger anything. Maybe the trigger is different - perhaps I need to move LEFT, or perhaps I need to reach the far left side of the sky blue region.
Let me try moving LEFT to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Moving RIGHT didn't trigger reconfiguration. Try LEFT instead.
# First check if board changed at all
seg = current_frame.segmentation
sky_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
for n in sky_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"Sky blue: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try moving LEFT to see if that triggers something
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns", "MOUSE": "Clicks on position - collects green blocks VERIFIED(step 2)", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns; triggered board reconfiguration once VERIFIED(step 14)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"background": "black with gray dots", "green_blocks": "light green 21-pixel blocks, collectable by MOUSE", "player": "blue+yellow small object", "platforms": "large sky blue regions"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "purple (left) + white (right) at row 63, likely progress/timer"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": "Green blocks respawn after board reconfiguration. Multiple phases possible within a level."}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1, phase 2 complete. All green blocks collected. Moving RIGHT 3 times did NOT trigger reconfiguration.", "goal_model": "Find what triggers next phase or completes level", "recent_findings": ["Moving RIGHT after collecting blocks did not trigger change"], "open_questions": ["What triggers phase transition?", "Does LEFT work instead?"], "current_plan": "Try moving LEFT to trigger next phase", "strategy_log": ["RIGHT after collecting blocks did nothing"], "failed_probes": ["ACTION7", "RIGHT after collecting blocks"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
